name: New Year
description: New Year traditions, songs, and celebrations around the world

questions.q: The song 'Auld Lang Syne' traditionally sung on New Year's Eve was written by which Scottish poet?

source.notes: Burns adapted traditional Scottish folk lyrics in 1788. Means 'old long since' or 'times gone by.'

questions.q: Where does the famous Times Square New Year's Eve ball drop take place?

source.notes: Tradition began 1907. Ball drops at midnight EST on December 31.

questions.q: In Spain and many Spanish-speaking countries, what do people eat at midnight on New Year's Eve, one for each chime?

source.notes: One grape per each of the 12 bell chimes. Each grape represents good luck for a month of the new year.

questions.q: What is the name of the ancient Roman god from whom January takes its name?

source.notes: Two-faced god — looks to the past and future. Also god of beginnings and transitions.

questions.q: In the Chinese zodiac, how many animals are there?

source.notes: Rat, Ox, Tiger, Rabbit, Dragon, Snake, Horse, Goat, Monkey, Rooster, Dog, Pig.

questions.q: The tradition of making New Year's resolutions dates back to which ancient civilization?

source.notes: ~4,000 years ago. Originally vows to gods to pay debts and return borrowed items.

questions.q: In Scotland, what is the name of the New Year's Eve celebration?

source.notes: Word origin unclear. Traditions include 'first-footing' (first visitor after midnight).

questions.q: In many Latin American countries, wearing what color underwear is said to bring love in the new year?

source.notes: Yellow brings wealth, red brings love/passion, white brings peace.

questions.q: Which Chinese New Year tradition involves a colorful performance often seen outside businesses?

source.notes: Both dragon and lion dances are common. Meant to bring good luck and ward off evil spirits.

questions.q: The Jewish New Year celebration is called what?

source.notes: Literally 'head of the year.' Apples dipped in honey symbolize a sweet new year.

questions.q: The Vietnamese New Year is called what?

source.notes: Full name 'Tết Nguyên Đán.' Celebrated on the same date as Chinese New Year.

questions.q: Persian New Year (the first day of spring) is called what?

source.notes: 'New Day' in Persian. Celebrated on the spring equinox (~March 20-21). Pre-Islamic tradition.

questions.q: The first Times Square New Year's Eve ball was built in what year?

source.notes: First dropped at midnight between December 31, 1907 and January 1, 1908. Weighed 700 lbs.

questions.q: Which TV broadcast led by Dick Clark (and later Ryan Seacrest) is a New Year's Eve staple?

source.notes: Full name: 'Dick Clark's New Year's Rockin' Eve.' Aired since 1972.

questions.q: A popular New Year's dish in the Southern US that's believed to bring luck is what?

source.notes: Often served with collard greens (for money) and cornbread (for gold).

questions.q: In the Gregorian calendar, how many days are there in a regular year?

source.notes: 366 in leap years (every 4 years, with exceptions).

questions.q: The Rose Parade takes place every January 1st in which US city?

source.notes: Preceded by the Rose Bowl college football game. Tradition since 1890.

questions.q: Which country officially introduced the Gregorian calendar in 1582?

source.notes: Introduced by Pope Gregory XIII. Replaced the Julian calendar. England didn't adopt until 1752.

questions.q: What kitchen item do the Danish traditionally break on a friend's doorstep on New Year's Eve to bring luck?

source.notes: Also save up broken plates year-round to throw on New Year's Eve. Sign of friendship.

questions.q: In many cultures, what two colors are considered especially lucky for New Year's celebrations?

source.notes: Especially common in Chinese tradition. Red = fortune/joy; gold = wealth/prosperity.
